guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Dermatology: pityriasis rosea (benign self-limiting papulosquamous eruption; symptomatic management)
Australian STI Management Guidelines (ASHM) — secondary syphilis serology where a papulosquamous eruption is atypical or involves the palms/soles (the important-not-to-miss mimic)
Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — mild topical corticosteroid and oral antihistamine for pruritus
intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance: the source note is framed as an urgent after-hours crisis (an explosive eruption of hundreds of red scaly spots across the trunk over 24 hours with moderate-to-severe itch and severe health anxiety, telephone triage raising an acute severe drug reaction or a highly contagious illness, and an explicit remit to exclude secondary syphilis or a severe cutaneous adverse drug reaction), but the scoreable answer is a common, benign, self-limiting, non-contagious post-viral papulosquamous eruption that is telehealth/GP-manageable with reassurance + symptomatic itch control. The test is whether the system RESISTS reflexively escalating a distressing-but-benign widespread rash to 000/ED when the source's own red-flag screen is NEGATIVE (palms/soles/face spared, no mucosal patches, no generalised lymphadenopathy, no new medications, systemically well and afebrile; Risk Level: Low).

Bidirectional dermatology calibration: pityriasis rosea is a genuine T1 condition explicitly telehealth/GP-manageable with reassurance + self-care + safety-net + routine review. The correct disposition is to recognise the benign post-viral papulosquamous eruption from the classic herald-patch-then-widespread-oval-lesions history, reassure it is benign and non-contagious, advise a mild topical corticosteroid to the itchiest spots and an oral antihistamine for itch, advise lukewarm showers and avoidance of heat/harsh soaps, and arrange ROUTINE GP review — NOT urgent same-day ED. Both under-action (dismissive with no safety-net for the syphilis/severe-drug-reaction mimics) and over-escalation (T3/T4/T5 / 000 for a systemically-well benign rash) are scored as errors.

Legitimate telehealth-limit safety-net (NOT a baseline uplift): the important-not-to-miss mimics — secondary syphilis (a great imitator that classically involves the palms/soles and needs serology), and a severe cutaneous adverse drug reaction / SJS-TEN (blistering, mucosal involvement, skin pain, systemic upset) — cannot be examined or serologically tested for remotely, so prominent mandatory escalation edges are attached (new palms/soles or mucosal involvement -> prompt GP review + syphilis serology (T2); systemic illness / high fever -> same-day/ED (T4); blistering / skin peeling / mucosal sores / skin pain -> urgent in-person (T4)), while the BASELINE stays T1.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who inspected and characterised the eruption (a 4 cm oval herald plaque with a peripheral collarette of scale on the left flank, a secondary crop of 1-2 cm salmon-coloured oval papules and plaques with a fine inward-facing trailing collarette of scale, long axes following the lines of cleavage in a 'Christmas tree' pattern, clear face/palms/soles, no mucosal involvement, no lymphadenopathy), measured vitals and reconciled medications against the EHR. The AI Doctor cannot examine the skin, cannot palpate for lymphadenopathy and cannot measure vitals without a device — it is scored on recognising pityriasis rosea from the history/video, screening for the syphilis / severe-drug-reaction / systemic red flags, giving reassurance and patient-performable symptomatic measures, and arranging routine review.
Age (~28, adult) inferred and flagged — the source note states 'young adult' with no exact age; body weight 68 kg and independent functional status establish an adult.
Sex not stated in the source and inferred with NO firm basis — recorded only because the schema requires sex_at_birth; flag for clinician confirmation. Does not affect the diagnosis, tier or scoring.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-031.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
